one_line_takeaway: Adding an average-embedding-directed regularizer to continuous-space suffix optimization lets a white-box attack jailbreak aligned LLMs into valid discrete tokens, beating or matching GCG/AutoDAN/PGD/SoftPromptThreats on ASR while running about 100x faster than GCG.
key_finding: Aggregated across 4 datasets, RR's overall ASR[>10] beats all four baselines on 3 of 5 models and ties GCG on the other 2. On Llama2-7B-chat RR reaches 31.5% ASR[>10] / 38% ASR[>5] versus GCG 10%/13.5%, AutoDAN 18%/25.5%, PGD 7%/8.5%, and SoftPromptThreats 5%/6%. On Vicuna-7B-v1.5 RR is 48%/63% (best; AutoDAN 41%/49.5%, GCG 21%/25.5%). On Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 RR is 60.5%/67% (best; AutoDAN 55%/66%, GCG 45%/49.5%). On Falcon-7B-Instruct RR ties GCG on ASR[>10] (both 45.5%) but leads on ASR[>5] (54.5% vs 47.5%). On MPT-7B-Chat RR ties GCG on ASR[>10] (both 42%) and leads on ASR[>5] (52% vs 43%). RR is roughly two orders of magnitude faster than GCG in average runtime (Fig. 3, log scale). A weight-decay ablation (Table 3) shows regularization drives the gains: raising weight decay from 0.0 to 0.1 lifts Llama2 overall ASR[>10] from 26.5% to 34.5% and Vicuna from 17.5% to 36% (ASR[>5] 19.5% to 58.5%).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Regularized Relaxation (RR), a white-box adversarial-suffix attack that jailbreaks aligned open-source LLMs by optimizing relaxed (continuous) suffix token embeddings with gradient descent, then discretizing them into valid vocabulary tokens. The novel element versus prior continuous-space work (Schwinn et al. 2024 / SoftPromptThreats, which optimizes embeddings but does NOT yield usable discrete tokens) is an L2-style regularization term that pulls the optimized suffix embeddings toward the model's average token embedding e-bar = (1/V) sum_i e_i. Because most of a model's token embeddings cluster tightly near this average (shown for Llama2-7B-chat, 32000 tokens / 4096 dims), the regularizer keeps optimized embeddings near valid tokens along the optimization path, so nearest-neighbor discretization produces effective adversarial suffixes. The authors claim ~two orders of magnitude speedup over GCG (Zou et al. 2023) plus higher attack success, while (unlike SoftPromptThreats) generating diverse, usable discrete tokens for prompts fed to public-facing models that only accept discrete input.
threat_model: White-box adversary with full access to an open-source LLM's weights, tokens, and embedding matrix, able to compute gradients. The attacker appends a 20-token adversarial suffix to a harmful-behavior prompt and optimizes it to maximize the likelihood of the model emitting a target harmful response (i.e., jailbreaking a safety-aligned model). The paper explicitly notes the method requires open-source/white-box access and its effectiveness in a black-box setting is untested; transferability to other victim models is examined separately (Table 2).
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) white-box only -- requires open-source access to embeddings and parameters; black-box effectiveness is unexplored. (2) Only 5 models and 4 datasets, which have marginal overlap in harmful behaviors; larger/more diverse datasets would strengthen conclusions. (3) The generated adversarial tokens, though valid, are human-unintelligible and can be caught by existing defenses (perplexity filtering, preprocessing, adversarial training) -- evading these is left to future work. Reviewer-noted caveats: only 50 behaviors per dataset are evaluated; absolute ASRs are modest (mostly 30-67%) under the authors' deliberately stricter dual-evaluator criterion; PGD was self-reimplemented (no public code) which introduces reproduction uncertainty; and the two-order-of-magnitude speedup vs GCG is not the same as vs the continuous-space SoftPromptThreats baseline it most closely builds on.

quantitative_results: Llama2-7B-chat overall: RR 31.5% ASR[>10] / 38% ASR[>5] vs GCG 10%/13.5%, AutoDAN 18%/25.5%, PGD 7%/8.5%, SoftPromptThreats 5%/6%
Vicuna-7B-v1.5 overall: RR 48% ASR[>10] / 63% ASR[>5] vs AutoDAN 41%/49.5%, GCG 21%/25.5%, PGD 18.5%/29.5%, SoftPromptThreats 14%/19%
Falcon-7B-Instruct overall: RR 45.5% ASR[>10] / 54.5% ASR[>5] vs GCG 45.5%/47.5%, AutoDAN 38%/43%
Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 overall: RR 60.5% ASR[>10] / 67% ASR[>5] vs AutoDAN 55%/66%, GCG 45%/49.5%
MPT-7B-Chat overall: RR 42% ASR[>10] / 52% ASR[>5] vs GCG 42%/43%, AutoDAN 41%/46.5%
Runtime: RR is approximately two orders of magnitude (~100x) faster than GCG (Fig. 3, averaged over all models/datasets)
Weight-decay ablation (Table 3): Llama2 overall ASR[>10] 26.5% (wd=0.0) -> 34.5% (wd=0.1); Vicuna 17.5% -> 36% ASR[>10] and 19.5% -> 58.5% ASR[>5]
Transfer (Table 2): Vicuna-source suffixes hit Mistral at 36.5% ASR[>10] / 43% ASR[>5]; Llama2-source suffixes hit Mistral at 35% / 38.5%
Hyperparameters: lambda = 0.05, 250 steps, 20-token '!' suffix init, single RTX A6000, half-precision
baselines_compared: GCG - Greedy Coordinate Gradient (Zou et al. 2023), discrete-token optimization
PGD (Geisler et al. 2024), projected gradient descent on one-hot encodings (self-reimplemented, no public code)
SoftPromptThreats (Schwinn et al. 2024), continuous embedding-space optimization (given identical discretization step for comparison)
